sentences.text: After that attempt to assassinate Hitler, attempt that was in vain, we somehow knew that Plaszow cannot be long as a camp, because we heard rumors that the Russian front is moving so fast that they are supposedly on the Vistula River, and we're very close, come here.
So we knew that they are preparing, and in the meantime, different things happened in Plaszow.
First of all, that lager elder and all the other people around him were shot by Amon Goeth.

They knew too much, and he knew too much about them, and they knew that Amon Goeth stole so many things from Plaszow and, instead of sending them to Berlin, he sent them to Czechoslovakia for himself.
So anyway, we couldn't believe but it was true.
Amon Goeth was arrested and taken out of that camp.
But we had to go.
We knew that they were sending men's transport to Gross-Rosen first and then--
Yeah.
So then, first the men were sent away.
And my brother was still staying in with the hospital in Plaszow.
And then there was a transport for women.
And actually, I did not have to go to this transport, but I didn't want to left my mother and my sister-in-law by themselves.
So I asked to be written to this transport.
And OK, I went.
Well, that person, that Buttner, who was the head of the clinic, he signed my name to this list.
By train, of course.
Nobody was counting.
But it was not too many.
They were cattle cars, and we had enough space to comfortably sit.
It was about quite a few hours, although it shouldn't be because Auschwitz is not so far away from Krakau.
We knew that we are going to Auschwitz.
We knew.
Openly, it was said, this transport is going to Auschwitz.
Period.
No, well, I heard that there are bad things going about in Auschwitz.
But I did not know.
At least, I didn't know-- we did not realize what.
The moment, in the evening, we came to the ramp, and the train stopped, and they start yelling and pulling everybody down, down, down.
And that train backed.
And when the train moved backwards, then I noticed-- this was evening already-- big chimneys and pfff, fire up to the sky.
And then I looked to my mother and said, so it's really true-- because it was smell, also, bad-- that they are burning people here.
One should come and see with one's own eyes to believe it.
And my mother said, don't look there, but look under your feet so that you shouldn't stumble.
And here, have a piece of sugar and eat before they will take away.
This was my mother.
She always wanted to keep us like this.
So we eventually moved out.
And we were marching through that birch forest.
And that is the name-- birch, [POLISH] Birkenau, in Polish [POLISH] from this woods.
And there we were, in front of this famous sauna.
But the Germans-- we stayed all night there and nobody knew what was going on.
And the next day, they put us all into the sauna naked.
It was Mengele who was doing that-- You saw him?
Yeah, sure.
He was standing in the doors, and he was saying, right left, right, left, while doing the selection, before we moved from one room into another.
Very big sauna.
Not after.
At the sauna-- sauna building.
First it was a hallway.
We were naked.
And there he was staying.
I was behind my mother, because I thought that, wherever my mother will be sent, I will go with her there without going through the selection.
And at this time, we already noticed that the old, or people who didn't look so well, they were staying there.
The others were over there.
So OK.
We passed that selection, all three of us.
Then they came.
They started shaving us all over.
And we went to another big cell, and there was [WHISPERS] quiet, because they closed the door.
And this was an enormous room, and we were there.
And suddenly, from the ceiling, came water.
And everybody yelled, water, because it was water and not gas.
And then, so after this sauna, we were given some funny-looking things.
I just got, I don't know, well, men's underwear or blouses of pajamas.
And we were marching to three or four camps, because Auschwitz was very large as a camp.
It had different camps, and ours was II B2.
And into this barrack.
And then we were doing nothing.
We were just standing on the appell, roll calls.
No numbers, nothing.
And then, it was like a miracle.
In our barrack, as a worker for that elder, it was a woman who used to do sewing, before the war, in our home.
And she, right away, took us under her wing.
And she gave us something to eat, and what else to wear.
And then she said, OK, you should try to get your number, because unless you have that number on your forearm, then you are in more danger to get selected and go to gas.
She was working for-- Reginka was her first name.
She was coming to our home and was doing some sewing before the war.
Yes, sure.
She was Jewish.
She was there much earlier, long ago.
And then, anyway, then there came a selection, and they were selecting people.
And they selected my mother as a clerk, and me, to any work.
And then right away, they took us into another barrack.
We came to another barrack, and I see my mother's not there.
And then, I thought, no way.
I have to go.
And I don't know how.
Maybe this was really the dear Lord who gave me-- like that nobody could see me.
I went straight to that Hoessler, who was lagerkommandant, and I told him in German that here is some wonderful secretary or clerk or whatever, my mother, and she is left in that other barrack.
And he said, well, get her number to the scribe, and we will bring her.
And I knew that my mother had no number and nothing.
But when that Slovakian girl asked me, what did he-- I said, he told me to run and bring my mother back.
And I didn't even wait for them.
I ran to that other barrack.
I grabbed my mother.
And this was absolutely-- I was the only one in this whole area, because you were not allowed to move in between the barracks.
So anyway, I was able to grab my mother, bring her there, pushed her in between others, and we got our number.
And then, right away--
Beautiful number, can read both ways the same, A-26462.
OK.
This way?
Anyway, and they transported us to Auschwitz I, what was the central Auschwitz there.
And then I was selected two days later, to be in the central files in Auschwitz [GERMAN].
There, I was in care of [GERMAN]-- of the files of the people who had been sent already to gas.
And people who were already in that evidence of that camp-- any camp, because Gross-Rosen or something, and came there, they came with their files.
We also came with our files-- Plaszow.

But then, when I had to look into that [GERMAN] where, for example, when there was a transport coming from, I don't know, Turkey or Hungary or something, it was only written, '4,000 people' or '3,000 people' [GERMAN].
And then I noticed there was a part of cards, and this was the transport from Plaszow, and there was my father.
And I found my father's card there.
And therefore, I know exactly when he was [GERMAN].
So I was working there.
And then I had another--
Beg your pardon?
This was November.
This was November '44.
Oh, the date of my father?
It was sometimes 15 that [GERMAN].
Sometime October.
Until the end in Auschwitz.
Birkenau, it was part of Auschwitz.
We were about two weeks in Birkenau, perhaps.
Auschwitz till 18th of January.
When Krakau was already-- 1945.

And so there, I was witnessing also, just at the beginning of January, the execution of the four Jewish girls who were supplying the sonderkommando with dynamite for the revolt of that sonderkommando who was working in the crematorium, and they sprang with dynamite.
Successfully.
And this crematorium-- and maybe this was also the reason that we went to work, and we were staying alive from Plaszow, because there was the largest crematorium didn't exist.
And they had such a backlog of dead people that they couldn't do it.
The girls were working at the munitions factory in Monowitz in one of these Auschwitz camps.
And they smuggled inside bread, for a long period of time, to these boys who were working in crematorium.
It was, perhaps, 8th or 10th of January.
I witnessed this.
I saw the two-- how do you say [POLISH] where you hang people?
Gallows-- were brought to the women [GERMAN].
In the meantime, first they hanged these two and then that was an air-raid, the Russian air-raid.
And they sent us all to basement.
And we were praying that they should bomb the whole camp.
But they didn't.
And then we came again, and we were seeing where-- the last one, the other girl, just before she was hanged, she screamed so loud in Polish language, [POLISH], what means vengeance.
I still hear it.
They were the four--
One of them.
Their names are known.
And then, a few days later, Auschwitz was liquidated and evacuated.
And we went on a four-days and four-nights walk, terrible walk, in day and night, day and night, till Wodzislaw.
This was a small station, where the tracks had all cattlecars.
And then there were all transports going, men to Mauthausen and women everywhere.
So we were on this open cattle for about maybe two weeks or three weeks, through all Germany.
Well, we were stopping in Gross-Rosen.
Open.
Yeah.
We just got one bread on this.
No, piece.
Each person got a piece of bread, and this was it.
No.
No.
Snow.
And do you know, this was strange, because it was open car, and we were just like this.
There was a soldier who was in the same car.
He had a big dog, wolfhound.
I love dogs, and he noticed that I love dogs, and he came.
And he was lying on my legs.
I couldn't move but I was, at least, warm.
No.
Nothing.
You see, but this is it.
For these two weeks, we--
We had nothing.
We did not eat, we did not drink, we did not need anything.
We just--
No.
A lot of people died.
Right there in front of you.
We were in a circle with young girls.
So somehow, there was still life in us.
But when we arrived in Ravensbruck, finally, well, then we noticed that the first things-- I get down, and the first thing they told us to pull the dead people out of this car.
And because I am tall, they told me-- I couldn't.
I fainted away.
Blocks.
We were living in blocks, not in barracks.
And the blocks, particularly that block which was office blocks, because there were living only people-- like I was working in hospitals or in other things, and we were in contact with the Germans.
So this was a spotless block.
I knew that there is resistance internationally, and I knew from my Polish friends who were working also.

As a matter of fact, three of them who were working with me, they saved Auschwitz for posterity, because they were the very first ones who came back from Mauthausen-- Erwin Olszowka and George-- I forgot his Polish name.
And they were fighting with people around Auschwitz, who wanted to dig, destroy, take--
Erwin.
He died two years ago.
E-R-W-E-N.
Olszowka.
He is this guy who gave me that little red notebook.
O-L-S-Z-O-W-K-A.
Well, the resourcefulness was like this, as far as I know, but not everybody was in such a situation.
Well, the international word in Auschwitz was [POLISH], Polish word that means 'darling,' because the first people who were in Auschwitz, they were Polish men.
And later on, when the women came, the men were helping women.
I was also getting some for someone whom I did not know.
Never knew.
Some food, some letters, something.
So this was it.
Yeah.
Messages, there was international-- Polish prime minister after the war, Jozef Cyrankiewicz, he was the head of the international resistances.
Messages, I don't know.
After all, the Germans had radio, and whenever Germans were saying that they had to strategically move from there, we knew exactly that they are running the hell out of it.
You see.
This is one thing.
About food, there was a kitchen.
People were working in the kitchen for SS, for the soldiers, and this.
And they were stealing.
For themselves, for friends, and for sale, sometimes.
Oh, it was a big black market.
People paid with exchange.
There was even a German nice song--
Yeah.
[SPEAKING GERMAN].
When we are going to pay with francs and marks instead of a little piece of bread and a lot of fighting?
In Birkenau, it was terrible.
In Birkenau, it was terrible.
And then my mother said, instead-- first of all, we couldn't even go to the bathroom.
So my mother said-- it was snow on the ground-- let's take that snow and let's wash yourself.
And we are not going to be clean of it, but when you forget to do it, you are lost.
And I know what she meant.
She meant that inner discipline.
No matter what, keep yourself in inner discipline.
Without this, you are lost.
And she was right.
This was Birkenau.
In Auschwitz I, there was a regular bathroom, and there was better condition to wash oneself.
This was the most important thing, because she did not talk in big words.
But I realized that once you lost this inner discipline, really, inner discipline, get up, get washed, no matter how incredible situation, try to keep normal life and normal values.
Stick to this.
It was Russian bread, and a piece of margarine or a sausage, soup, something like that.
I was working with these files.
Yeah.
Regular sleep.
At 10:00 we were in bed, and we got up at 6:00.
Of course, we were going to roll call.
All the time.
Roll call was in the morning and roll call was in the evening.
And we were hardly inside the barrack.
But the barrack was spotless.
Not barrack-- the block.
And it's still there.
That block was spotless.
My mother, when she was with me, she just put on me the blanket and told me to go out in the evening for a few minutes so that the bedding can air.
And everybody took this from us.
No.
The only holiday which could be at this time was Hanukkah, but nobody was doing this.
Oh, yeah.

Especially we had day, because every day they came-- papers from [GERMAN] or from the scribe, that everybody is-- so many and so many people working this kommando, or so many and so many people were [GERMAN], because they were still gassing people, but not in the big transports.
And then on 18th of January, we were chased away.
And then suddenly, we arrived eventually in Ravensbruck.
And from Ravensbruck, I remember very little, except at the beginning dragging the dead people from there.
Because I got typhoid fever.
None.
No medical attention.
Nothing.
I don't know.
Not everybody died.
No.
I did not know.
I was out of it.
I was in fever.
When I was a little bit out, I tried to write some poems.
And then, while before I was always fighting, actually, with good Lord, then I finally said, I can't any more.
Please help me.
Please save me.
I don't remember exactly.
I know that, at the end of February or beginning of March, I was in Malchow.
They transported us there.
Probably February.
